context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the spider diagram for my city case study. I read the assignment, but I still don’t really get what I’m supposed to put in each part or how detailed it should be.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's get you oriented. Before we dig into the diagram itself, tell me: what do you know so far about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation mentioned in the assignment? Even a rough idea of what they are would be a great starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning of the assignment and feeling lost. Rather than explaining the spider diagram structure for them, I should use Socratic questioning to help them activate their own understanding. I'll start by asking what they do know - specifically about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation, since that's the core framework the spider diagram is built around. This will help me gauge where they actually are and what they need most. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Honestly not much — I remember there are five categories, but they’re all blending together for me. I’m not even sure if I’m supposed to list policies, people, problems, or like outcomes under each one.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good honest starting point! Before we go further, I'd encourage you to go back to your course materials for this week - the 5 Pathways should be defined there. Once you have them in front of you, come back and tell me what they are in your own words. That foundation is really what the whole diagram hangs on.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being honest about not remembering the 5 Pathways, which is a foundational knowledge gap that needs to be addressed before they can meaningfully work on the spider diagram. However, I shouldn't just list the pathways for them - I should prompt them to go back to their course materials. This is a retrieval issue, not a conceptual one. A short, direct nudge to their course resources is appropriate here. I'll stay Socratic and ask them to look it up rather than providing it.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s part of why I’m stuck — when I look at the course page, it still feels too abstract. I think I need a simpler big-picture explanation of what the spider diagram is even trying to show first, because right now all the pieces just feel disconnected.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough - let's think about it together rather than me just telling you. The assignment says you're mapping 'climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways.' So ask yourself: what would be the point of mapping your city's actions against a set of categories? What does that kind of exercise usually help you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the spider diagram concept for them, which risks me doing the intellectual work they should be doing. However, they are expressing genuine confusion about the big picture, which is a legitimate struggle. I can help them reason toward the purpose of the diagram through a Socratic question rather than explaining it. I'll ask them to think about what the assignment is asking them to do at a high level - mapping climate action in their city against the 5 pathways - and prompt them to infer the purpose from that description alone.

exchanges.student: Maybe it’s supposed to show whether the city is doing a lot or a little? Or where stuff fits? I’m not sure though, because then I get confused about whether I’m categorizing actions, stakeholders, or climate risks, and I start mixing all of them together.
exchanges.tutor: You're on to something with 'a lot or a little' - that's actually closer than you think. Now, the assignment title itself gives you a big clue about what the main focus should be. It says 'City Case Study Investigation' about 'climate action.' So if climate action is the focus, what do you think should be the primary thing you're putting into each pathway - actions, stakeholders, or risks?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good intuitive progress - their guess about showing 'a lot or a little' is actually pointing in the right direction toward the comparative/evaluative function of a spider diagram. I should affirm that direction without confirming it outright, and then help them resolve their confusion about what goes in the diagram. Rather than listing what belongs, I'll ask them a focused question that helps them reason it out: the assignment title itself says 'climate action,' which is a useful anchor. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess actions, but then I feel like I’m guessing and I don’t know how much background I’m supposed to include. Like if my city has a transit plan and a flood plan and some emissions goal, do I just dump those in, or am I supposed to explain who’s involved and why they matter too?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on actions! Now think about the format itself: a spider diagram is a mapping tool, not an essay. Given that, do you think it's designed to hold lots of detailed explanation, or to give a structured overview? And have you looked closely at the example table in the assignment - what does it show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified 'actions' as the primary focus, which is good progress. Now they're asking a more nuanced question about depth and what to include alongside the actions. This is a productive question. Rather than telling them how detailed to be, I can prompt them to think about what a spider diagram is designed to show - it's a visual mapping tool, not an essay. That should help them calibrate. I'll also gently point them toward the assignment description itself for guidance on format, since it mentions the table option with examples. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I looked at it, but I still kind of blanked. I think that’s what’s tripping me up — I can’t tell what counts as a “structured overview” versus leaving out too much, and then I start worrying I’m organizing it wrong before I even start.
exchanges.tutor: It sounds like you're overthinking the setup before you've tried anything. You already mentioned your city has a transit plan, a flood plan, and an emissions goal — that's real material! Pick just one of those and try to figure out which of the 5 Pathways it might belong under. What's your city, and which action do you want to start with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student seems to be in a bit of an anxiety loop about getting it perfect before starting. This is a common freshman pattern - paralysis before beginning. The best move here is to push them to just start with what they have, rather than continuing to discuss the format abstractly. They've identified their city has a transit plan, a flood plan, and emissions goals - that's already material to work with. I should redirect them to take a concrete first step: pick one of those actions and try to place it under one of the 5 pathways. This will get them unstuck without me giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: My city is Boston, and that’s where I get more stuck because it feels like one action could fit multiple pathways. The transit stuff seems like emissions reduction, resilience, governance, maybe equity too, so then I don’t know how to place anything without overexplaining all of it.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really good observation, and it shows you're thinking carefully. But here's a question: does Boston's transit plan exist primarily to cut emissions, or primarily to move people around equitably, or something else? What would you say is its main goal? That should help you decide where it fits most naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely insightful observation - actions can span multiple pathways. This is actually a sophisticated realization, not a mistake. I should affirm this briefly and then help them see that the spider diagram doesn't require a single 'correct' placement - the point is to map what's most prominent or central. I should ask them to think about what the transit plan is primarily trying to achieve, which will help them make a defensible placement choice. This keeps them moving forward without me doing the categorization for them.

exchanges.student: That’s exactly where I spiral, because I feel like city plans always claim like five main goals at once. I think I need a really plain example of how one Boston action would get placed in just one pathway without writing a whole justification for every other possible pathway.
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration, but giving you an example placement would take away exactly the thinking this assignment wants you to do. Here's the thing: making a judgment call when multiple pathways fit IS the skill being practiced. What do you think is Boston's transit plan's single strongest connection? Commit to one and we'll go from there — you can always note secondary connections briefly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now explicitly asking me to give them an example placement, which would essentially be doing the work for them. I need to hold my boundary here - I cannot model a complete placement for them. However, they are expressing genuine frustration and going in circles on this categorization question. I should acknowledge the frustration briefly but firmly redirect. The key insight they need is that in a mapping exercise, making a reasonable judgment call IS the skill - there's no perfect answer. I should push them to just commit to a placement and move on, rather than seeking certainty first.

exchanges.student: I still don’t really know what “strongest connection” means here, and I’m worried I’m building the whole diagram on a shaky choice. Could you just give me the simplest possible big-picture explanation of what the finished spider diagram is supposed to let someone see at a glance?
exchanges.tutor: I notice we keep circling back to the same question instead of making any progress on the diagram itself. I think the best way for you to understand what it's supposed to show is to actually try filling something in - even imperfectly. Take Boston's transit plan, pick the one pathway you feel even slightly most drawn to, and write one sentence placing it there. What do you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me again to explain the big picture for them - this is the third time they've circled back to this meta-question about the diagram's purpose rather than attempting any actual work. This is a redundant pattern. I've been Socratic but they keep deflecting back to seeking explanation rather than trying. I should be more direct here: acknowledge we've been going in circles, and firmly push them to make a first attempt - even a rough, imperfect one. I should not explain the finished product for them. It's time to be less accommodating and push for action.

exchanges.student: I’d maybe put transit under a mitigation-type pathway because it can reduce car use and emissions, but I’m still not confident because that feels too simple and maybe I’m missing what the assignment wants.
exchanges.tutor: That's solid reasoning - you identified an action, connected it to an outcome, and made a defensible choice. 'Too simple' doesn't mean wrong; it might just mean clear. Now, instead of second-guessing this one, can you try the same move with your flood plan? What pathway does that point toward, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally made a concrete placement attempt - this is real progress and I should acknowledge it genuinely. Their reasoning is sound and they should feel more confident. However, they're second-guessing themselves, which is partly the anxiety pattern we've seen throughout. I should affirm the quality of their reasoning without confirming it's definitively 'correct,' and push them to keep building rather than dwelling on this one choice. We've now spent 9 exchanges mostly on setup - I should encourage them to start populating the rest of the diagram.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly circles back to the same confusion about how to place an action in a pathway, and the tutor cycles through similar Socratic prompts across turns 6–9 without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing, or simplifying. The tutor acknowledges the spiral in turn 9 but still does not switch to a substantially different approach (e.g., a concrete worked analogy, a simplified framework, or a direct reframe of the task). The conversation stalls on the same sub-point for four consecutive exchanges.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, never giving away answers or doing the student's reasoning for them, and consistently redirecting the student back to the assignment. The one clear weakness is in dialogue quality: the student spirals on the same confusion across turns 6–9, and the tutor's responses, while individually reasonable, do not sufficiently reframe or synthesize to break the loop. The tutor acknowledges the spiral in turn 9 but does not pivot to a meaningfully different strategy. All other criteria are well-handled.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps key idea generation student-led. Scaffolding (1.2) is solid: the tutor builds on student progress (e.g., affirming the transit/mitigation connection in turn 10) and asks diagnostic questions before responding. Meta-learning (1.3) is adequately present, particularly in turn 8 where the tutor explicitly names 'making a judgment call when multiple pathways fit IS the skill being practiced.' The 4-point deduction under 2.1 is warranted because the student's core confusion about pathway placement persists across turns 6–9 with the tutor cycling through similar prompting moves without a meaningful reframe, simplification, or synthesis that would break the loop. Assignment anchoring (2.2) and communication quality (3.1, 3.2) are strong throughout.
